facts.fact: In 2024, raw sewage poured into England's rivers and seas for a record 3.61 million hours.
There were 450,398 sewage spill events in England in 2024 — more than 1,200 every single day.
The 2024 sewage spill total is equivalent to 412 continuous years of raw sewage flowing into our waterways.
Across the UK in 2024, there was an estimated sewage discharge almost every 30 seconds.
Thames Water alone discharged raw sewage for 298,081 hours in 2024 — a 51% rise on the year before.
Every day in the UK, over 11 billion litres of wastewater flows through 347,000 km of sewers.
England has over 15,000 Combined Sewer Overflow sites discharging directly into rivers and seas.
Water companies are legally permitted to discharge untreated sewage into rivers — they just aren't supposed to do it routinely.
About 30% of Thames Water's underground infrastructure is unmapped — vast sections are unknown even to the company.
Most sewage treatment permits do not require the removal of dangerous pathogens — even 'treated' effluent still threatens public health.
Not a single stretch of river in England or Northern Ireland is in good overall health.
facts.source: Rivers Trust State of Our Rivers Report 2024

facts.fact: Only 14% of rivers in England meet good ecological status. None pass good chemical status.
facts.source: Environment Agency / House of Lords Library

facts.fact: Toxic chemicals that persist for decades have been found in every single stretch of English river.
facts.source: Rivers Trust State of Our Rivers Report 2024

facts.fact: Three-quarters of shellfish waters in England fail to meet water quality standards.
Agriculture is responsible for 40% of England's river pollution — more than water companies.
Sewage triggers algal blooms that block sunlight, suffocate aquatic plants, and strip oxygen from the water.
When algae die and decompose, bacteria consume what little oxygen remains — killing fish and invertebrates.
facts.source: Natural History Museum / London Wildlife Trust

facts.fact: Freshwater insects are among the first to die when sewage enters a river — many spend years developing underwater.
When sewage destroys insect populations, fish lose their food source — even those that swam clear of the discharge.
Raw sewage contains E. coli, Enterococci, microplastics, PFAS 'forever chemicals', and antibiotic-resistant bacteria.
Microplastics and PFAS 'forever chemicals' from sewage cannot be removed once in the environment — they accumulate forever.
On the River Otter, E. coli exceeded the safe swimming limit on more than 90% of days monitored.
After rainfall, E. coli in some UK rivers spikes to 100 times the legal safe limit for swimming.
facts.source: Hansard / South West Water FOI data

facts.fact: A single sewage treatment works can increase phosphate levels in a river by around 80% during summer months.
Sewage pollution impacts the entire food chain — from aquatic plants and insects up to herons, otters, and seals.
Agriculture is responsible for 70% of the nitrogen and 25% of the phosphorus entering UK waterways.
Agriculture affects more than 60% of England's rivers that are failing to meet good ecological status.
facts.source: Parliamentary Office of Science and Technology

facts.fact: In 2021, agriculture accounted for 87% of all UK ammonia emissions.
Dairy farming alone caused 48 serious river pollution incidents in England in 2024.
The River Wye has lost 90% of its Ranunculus weed beds due to phosphorus runoff from intensive poultry farming.
Farmland in the River Wye catchment holds enough excess phosphorus to fertilise crops for another 20 years without further input.
Pesticides, herbicides, and fungicides from farms wash into rivers during rainfall — often impossible to trace back to source.
facts.source: Parliamentary Office of Science and Technology

facts.fact: Farm runoff drives up household water bills — water companies must spend heavily to remove agricultural nutrients before it reaches taps.
Antidepressants, antibiotics, painkillers, and contraceptive hormones have all been detected in UK rivers.
facts.source: Imperial College London / University of York

facts.fact: Pharmaceuticals were found in river water at 52 out of 54 locations monitored across all ten of England's National Parks.
Up to 90% of drugs consumed by humans pass through the body unchanged and enter the sewage system.
Only 27% of people return unused medicines to a pharmacy — the rest are flushed or binned, entering waterways.
Synthetic oestrogen from contraceptive pills has caused male fish to grow female organs and produce female hormones.
Antidepressants in river water have been shown to reduce fish feeding rates and alter risk-taking behaviour.
A London site had the highest recorded hazard level in a global study of antidepressant water pollution.
facts.source: Natural History Museum / global pharmaceutical study

facts.fact: Prescription drugs including antidepressants, diabetes drugs, and antibiotics have been found inside crabs and oysters off the English coast.
The UK has no legal requirement to systematically monitor pharmaceuticals in rivers, bathing waters, or drinking water sources.
Antibiotic pollution in rivers is accelerating the rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria — a threat to human medicine globally.
